FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; baseline 2016-2018, ages 9-10; N=9,208; ABCD 4.0 release from NIMH Data Archive

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective cohort analysis; logistic regression examining baseline screen time predicting 2-year follow-up OCD diagnosis

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Journal of Adolescent Health, 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time IV includes specific modalities: watching TV/movies, watching videos (YouTube), playing video games, texting, video chatting, social media (Facebook, Instagram, Twitter). OCD outcome measured via KSADS-5 is individual-trait-level psychiatric symptom. Multiple SM/phone modalities paired with individual-trait outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Adjusted odds ratios reported for SM/phone ↔ OCD relationship: aOR 1.05 (95% CI 1.01-1.09) per hour total screen time; aOR 1.15 (95% CI 1.03-1.28) for video games; aOR 1.11 (95% CI 1.01-1.23) for watching videos

FT_IC4.evidence: See FT_IC3; multiple numeric results across models (Models 1, 2, 3)

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal (baseline → 2-year follow-up); screen time is IV; OCD is DV. Criterion 3 (no SM-as-IV analysis) does NOT hold—screen time is analyzed as IV throughout. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Strong prospective design with diverse sample (48.9% female, 43.5% non-White). Screen time measured via ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey; OCD via KSADS-5 at follow-up. Adjusted for race/ethnicity, sex, household income, parent education, baseline depression, family history of psychopathology, study site.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; Year 2 (2018-2020), ages 10-14; N=10,280; cross-sectional from baseline cohort

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; modified Poisson regression examining associations between bedtime screen use and sleep outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Sleep Health: Journal of the National Sleep Foundation, 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Bedtime screen use IV includes: TV/movies, video games, music, phone calls/texting, social media, chat rooms, Internet browsing, computer/laptop studying, reading. Sleep outcomes (trouble falling/staying asleep, sleep disturbance via SDSC) are individual-trait-level behavioral/health outcomes. Device in bedroom and phone ringer status also examined as IV. Multiple SM/phone modalities paired with individual-trait outcomes.

FT_IC3.evidence: Adjusted risk ratios reported: ARR 1.27 (95% CI 1.12-1.44) for device in bedroom; ARR 1.23 (95% CI 1.03-1.46) for ringer on; ARR 1.15 (95% CI 1.09-1.21) for watching TV/movies; ARR 1.22 (95% CI 1.14-1.29) for video games; ARR 1.20 (95% CI 1.14-1.25) for music; ARR 1.16 (95% CI 1.09-1.23) for phone/texting; ARR 1.16 (95% CI 1.10-1.23) for social media; ARR 1.27 (95% CI 1.17-1.39) for chat rooms

FT_IC4.evidence: See FT_IC3; extensive numeric results across bedtime activities and overnight notifications

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional (Year 2 snapshot, 2018-2020); no baseline-to-follow-up temporal sequence. EC7 only applies to longitudinal designs. Does not apply.
notes: Diverse, large population-based sample (48.8% female, 47.6% racial/ethnic minority). Bedtime screen use measured via 9-item self-report questionnaire; sleep outcomes via KSADS DSM-5 item (self-reported) and Sleep Disturbance Scale for Children (caregiver-reported). Adjusted for sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parent education, depression, data collection period (pre- vs. during COVID-19), study site.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; baseline 2016-2018, ages 9-11; N=11,875; ABCD 3.0 release; 1-year follow-up 2017-2019, ages 10-11

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective cohort analysis; modified Poisson regression examining baseline screen time (total and by modality) predicting 1-year follow-up conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry, 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time IV includes: total screen time, social media, video chat (Skype, FaceTime), texting, television/movies, video games. Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder measured via KSADS-5 are individual-trait-level psychiatric diagnoses. Multiple SM/phone modalities paired with individual-trait outcomes.

FT_IC3.evidence: Prevalence ratios reported for SM/phone ↔ conduct/ODD relationships: PR 1.07 (95% CI 1.03-1.11) per hour total screen time on conduct disorder; PR 1.62 (95% CI 1.39-1.87) for social media on conduct disorder; PR 1.21 (95% CI 1.06-1.37) for video chat on ODD; PR 1.19 (95% CI 1.07-1.33) for texting on ODD; PR 1.17 (95% CI 1.10-1.25) for TV/movies on ODD; PR 1.14 (95% CI 1.07-1.21) for video games on ODD

FT_IC4.evidence: See FT_IC3; multiple numeric results across screen modalities; also reports threshold effects (>4 hours total screen time associated with 69% higher prevalence of conduct disorder, 46% for ODD)

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal (baseline → 1-year follow-up); screen time is IV; disruptive behavior disorders are DV. Criterion 3 (no SM-as-IV analysis) does NOT hold—screen time is analyzed as IV throughout. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Large, prospective, nationally representative sample (N=11,875 at baseline). Diverse demographics reported at baseline. Screen time measured via ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey (self-reported); conduct/ODD via KSADS-5 parent-report at 1-year follow-up. Adjusted for potential confounders. Strongest association found between social media and conduct disorder (PR 1.62).
